洲崎神社 | image = Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Ama no Hirinome | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[:en:Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[:en:Bōsō Peninsula|Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Ishibashiyama|Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[:en:Heike clan|Heike clan]]. In the late [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[:en:Edo Bay|Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] }} [[sn:Susaki Shrine]] [[en:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] {{Commons category|Susaki-jinja}} [[ja:洲崎神社]]

洲崎神社 | image = Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Ama no Hirinome | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[:en:Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[:en:Bōsō Peninsula|Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Ishibashiyama|Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[:en:Heike clan|Heike clan]]. In the late [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[:en:Edo Bay|Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[:en:RoutledgeCurzon|RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] }} [[sn:Susaki Shrine]] [[en:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] {{Commons category|Susaki-jinja}} [[ja:洲崎神社]]

洲崎神社 | image = Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Ama no Hirinome | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[:en:Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[:en:Bōsō Peninsula|Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[:en:Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[:en:Tokyo Bay|Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Ishibashiyama|Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[:en:Heike clan|Heike clan]]. In the late [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[:en:Edo Bay|Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[:en:RoutledgeCurzon|RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] }} [[sn:Susaki Shrine]] [[en:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] {{Commons category|Susaki-jinja}} [[ja:洲崎神社]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11556513]] Wikidata name: Sunosaki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Susaki Jinja
洲崎神社 | image = File:Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.968056|N|139.758222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Q85879124|qid=Q85879124}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tateyama|qid=Q646755}} }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[:en:Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[:en:Bōsō Peninsula|Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[:en:Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[:en:Tokyo Bay|Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Ishibashiyama|Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[:en:Heike clan|Heike clan]]. In the late [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[:en:Edo Bay|Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[:en:RoutledgeCurzon|RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] }} [[sn:Susaki Shrine]] [[en:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] {{Commons category|Susaki-jinja}} [[ja:洲崎神社]] [[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11556513]] Wikidata name: Sunosaki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Susaki Jinja
洲崎神社 | image = File:Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.968056|N|139.758222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Q85879124|qid=Q85879124}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tateyama|qid=Q646755}} }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[:en:Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[:en:Bōsō Peninsula|Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[:en:Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[:en:Tokyo Bay|Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Ishibashiyama|Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[:en:Heike clan|Heike clan]]. In the late [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[:en:Edo Bay|Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[:en:RoutledgeCurzon|RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] }} [[en:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] {{Commons category|Susaki-jinja}} [[ja:洲崎神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11556513}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Susaki-jinja]][[en:Susaki Shrine]][[ja:洲崎神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11556513]] Wikidata name: Sunosaki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Susaki Jinja
洲崎神社 | image = File:Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.968056|N|139.758222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Q85879124|WD=Q85879124}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tateyama|WD=Q646755}} }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[:en:Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[:en:Bōsō Peninsula|Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[:en:Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[:en:Tokyo Bay|Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Ishibashiyama|Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[:en:Heike clan|Heike clan]]. In the late [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[:en:Edo Bay|Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[:en:RoutledgeCurzon|RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] }} [[en:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Susaki-jinja}} [[ja:洲崎神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11556513}}

(1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[:en:RoutledgeCurzon|RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' {{Infobox religious building | name = Susaki Jinja
洲崎神社 | image = Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Ama no Hirinome | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. In the late [[Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Susaki Shrine]] [[en:Susaki Shrine]]

Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. In the late [[Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. 洲崎神社 | image = Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Ama no Hirinome | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. In the late [[Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] }} [[sn:Susaki Shrine]] [[en:Susaki Shrine]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

'''Susaki Shrine''' is a shrine in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is located in [[Awa Province (Chiba)]]. == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable sortable" ! District !! Name !! Funding category !! Rank !! Notes !! Same as !! Co-ords !! Shrine DB |- | RONSHA || {{ill|Susaki Shrine|lt=Sunosaki Shrine|en|Susaki Shrine|ja|洲崎神社|commons|Category:Susaki-jinja|WD=Q11556513}} || — || — || {{ill|ichinomiya|WD=Q1656379|lt=ichinomiya}} || {{ill|Mimeno-amehirinohimeno- Shrine|lt=Mimeno-amehirinohimeno- Shrine|WD=Q135039751}} || {{coord|34.968122|139.758306|display=inline}} || — |} {{wikidata link|Q11556513}} [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[sn:2]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[sn:3]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[sn:4]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Susaki Jinja
洲崎神社 | image = Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Ama no Hirinome | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. In the late [[Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' revision.text:
'''Susaki Shrine''' is a shrine in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is located in [[Awa Province (Chiba)]]. == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable sortable" ! District !! Name !! Funding category !! Rank !! Notes !! Same as !! Co-ords !! Shrine DB |- | RONSHA || {{ill|Susaki Shrine|lt=Sunosaki Shrine|en|Susaki Shrine|ja|洲崎神社|commons|Category:Susaki-jinja|WD=Q11556513}} || — || — || {{ill|ichinomiya|WD=Q1656379|lt=ichinomiya}} || {{ill|Mimeno-amehirinohimeno- Shrine|lt=Mimeno-amehirinohimeno- Shrine|WD=Q135039751}} || {{coord|34.968122|139.758306|display=inline}} || — |} {{wikidata link|Q11556513}} [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[sn:2]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[sn:3]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[sn:4]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Susaki Jinja
洲崎神社 | image = Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Ama no Hirinome | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. In the late [[Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] }} [[sn:Susaki Shrine]] [[en:Susaki Shrine]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Susaki Jinja

In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. In the late [[Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. '''Susaki Shrine''' is a shrine in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is located in [[Awa Province (Chiba)]]. == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable sortable" ! District !! Name !! Funding category !! Rank !! Notes !! Same as !! Co-ords !! Shrine DB |- | RONSHA || {{ill|Susaki Shrine|lt=Sunosaki Shrine|en|Susaki Shrine|ja|洲崎神社|commons|Category:Susaki-jinja|WD=Q11556513}} || — || — || {{ill|ichinomiya|WD=Q1656379|lt=ichinomiya}} || {{ill|Mimeno-amehirinohimeno- Shrine|lt=Mimeno-amehirinohimeno- Shrine|WD=Q135039751}} || {{coord|34.968122|139.758306|display=inline}} || — |} {{wikidata link|Q11556513}} [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[sn:2]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[sn:3]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[sn:4]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[Category:Pages with merged translations]][[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Susaki Jinja
洲崎神社 | image = Susaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Susaki Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Ama no Hirinome | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = Susaki, Tateyama, Chiba | website = | architecture_style = | festival = August 10 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Susaki Shrine'''|洲崎神社|Susaki Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Susaki neighborhood of the city of [[Tateyama, Chiba|Tateyama]] in [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. In the late [[Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. In the late [[Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the former [[Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Susaki Jinja is: *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Hiritome-no-mikoto'''|天比理乃咩命}}, the consort of [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto]] who is worshipped at the [[Awa Shrine]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==History== Per the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' records dated 807 AD, early during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Jimmu]], Ame-no-Tomi, landed on the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in what is now the province of Awa in search of fertile land, and established a settlement together with the Inbe clan. He brought with him a mirror which had been owned by his grandmother, Ama no Hirinome, which he enshrined on Mount Mitarai, and this was the start of the Susaki Shrine. According to the shrine's legend, during the [[Asuka period]], the shrine was visited by [[En no Gyōja]] after a landslide had buried the "mirror pond" in the precincts, thus releasing a great snake which had to be vanquished. In addition, En no Gyōja placed one sacred stone on the coast in front of a [[torii]] gate which faced the coast, and another at the Awakuchi Shrine in [[Yokosuka, Kanagawa|Yokosuka]] on the opposite side of [[Tokyo Bay]] for maritime safety. These legends illustrate the close connection the shrine had with ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' and [[Shugendō]]. Since ancient times, the shrine had the right to collect toll taxes from passing ships.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is mentioned in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'', ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' and ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' as well as the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and although a relatively small shrine, had attained the highest Rank by 1081. This was partly due to the patronage of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who landed on the coast close to this shrine after his defeat at the [[Battle of Ishibashiyama]]. Per the [[Azuma Kagami]], he credited the shrine with his safe passage, and for helping rally the local samurai bands of the Bōsō Peninsula to his banner to overthrow the [[Heike clan]]. In the late [[Edo Period]], while surveying the coastline of [[Edo Bay]] with the purpose of establishing coastal defenses, the ''[[rōjū]]'' [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]] wrote an account in which he referred to the shine as the "Awa Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin". This is the first mention of the shrine as the "Ichinomiya" of the province. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Susaki Jinja was designated the Rank of prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] under [[State Shinto]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Gallery== 洲崎神社 (館山市) 浜鳥居.JPG|Seaside Torii 洲崎神社 (館山市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden 洲崎神社 (館山市) 随身門.JPG|Zuishin-mon 洲崎神社 (館山市) 神石.JPG|Sacred Stone ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Susaki-jinja}} *[http://tateyamacity.com/archives/2746 Tateyama City Tourist Association] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]]. {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ The Imperial House of Japan]''.'' Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Inbe Faith}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Tateyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Inbe clan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Susaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] }} [[sn:Susaki Shrine]] [[en:Susaki Shrine]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == {{Infobox religious building | name = Susaki Jinja
