Worship hall |Location = {{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q80011}}{{jalink|Tateyama City|Q646755}} Susaki 1344 |Coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deity = {{jalink|Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Major) {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}}
(Traditionally) {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Main hall = Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}}, copper roofing |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = {{jalink|August 20|Q2824}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=34|lat_min=58|lat_sec=5|lon_deg=139|lon_min=45|lon_sec=29.6|label=Susaki Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), otorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Great torii gate}}]] [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), hamatorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Beach torii}}{{small|Facing the coast. The peak on the left is Mitarai-yama.}}]] '''Susaki Shrine''' (すさきじんじゃ / すのさきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Susaki, {{jalink|Tateyama City|Q646755}}, {{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q80011}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (major shrine) {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}}, and during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was regarded as the {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}}, it was classified as a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. The shrine is enshrined on the mid-slope of Mount Mitarai. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is as follows: * '''{{jalink|Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}}''' (あまのひりのめのみこと){{refnest|group="Notes"|The name and reading of the deity follow the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine.}} *: She is the consort of {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}}’s enshrined deity {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ama-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}, and was originally called “Susa no Kami.” === Variations in the deity’s name === The notation of the deity’s name differs in historical sources as follows: * Amahiri'''no'''me-no-Mikoto *: According to the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}. Works such as {{jalink|Suzuka no Muraji Tane|Q6536446}}’s {{jalink|Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708}}, the {{jalink|Ministry of Doctrine|Q4294653}}-compiled {{jalink|Tokusen Jinmei-chō|Q11571092}}, and the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine follow this notation. * Amahiri'''to'''me-no-Mikoto (あめのひりとめのみこと) *: According to sources older than the Jinmyōchō, such as the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}, {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}, and {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}. Documents such as “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki” and “Sunomiya Shrine Records” record the deity under this name. It has been suggested that this discrepancy arose because the Jinmyōchō mistakenly wrote the character “乃” instead of “刀” for the name originally recorded as Amahiritome-no-Mikoto in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}Kenichi Tanigawa (ed.), *Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 11 Kantō* (Hakusuisha, December 1984). According to the “Kanemaru Family Genealogical Record” ({{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 [{{jalink|1597|Q6790}}]), the Sunomiya Kō Shrine in Sunomiya Village, Awa District, later called Sunomiya Myōjin, was regarded as the inner sanctuary and the second shrine, while Susaki Myōjin on Mount Mitarai in Susaki Village was regarded as the worship hall and the first shrine. Based on this, it has been proposed that Susaki Shrine and {{jalink|Sunomiya Shrine|Q11556507}} formed a paired shrine complex enshrining the “Susa no Kami,” with Susaki Shrine as the first shrine and Sunomiya Shrine as the secondMedieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Research Group (ed.), *Fundamental Research on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System* (Iwata Shoin, February 2000). === History === === Founding === According to the {{jalink|Kogo Shūi|Q531555}} ({{jalink|Daido|Q1373191}} 2 [{{jalink|807|Q30016}}]), {{jalink|Amenotomi-no-Mikoto|Q24899653}}, acting on the orders of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, landed in Awa Province in search of fertile land and developed it. He later led part of the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229}} to the {{jalink|Bōsō Peninsula|Q1022115}} in pursuit of even richer land. According to the shrine tradition “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki,” compiled in {{jalink|Hōreki|Q1194397}} 3 ({{jalink|1753|Q7621}}), Susaki Shrine originated when Amenotomi-no-Mikoto enshrined a mirror belonging to his ancestral grandmother deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto on Mitarasu-yama (Mount Mitarai). Shrine tradition also recounts that in Yōrō 1 ({{jalink|717|Q23532}}), a great earthquake buried Kanegae Pond within the precincts, and a great serpent guarding a subterranean bell caused calamities. {{jalink|En no Gyōja|Q198946}} is said to have performed prayers for seven days and nights, received a divine oracle, and subdued the serpent, thereby removing the disaster. It is also said that En no Gyōja placed sacred stones for maritime safety on the shore in front of the beach torii and at Awaguchi Shrine in {{jalink|Yokosuka City|Q200578|Yokosuka}}{{refnest|group="Notes"|The paired sacred stone is enshrined as the sacred object of Awaguchi Shrine in Yoshii, Yokosuka City.}}. These legends indicate that Susaki Shrine was strongly influenced by {{jalink|Shugendō|Q1483875}} and {{jalink|Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088}} from early times. === Overview === ==== Medieval period ==== “Awa Province Amahiritome-no-Kami” appears frequently in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168}} and received successive promotions in {{jalink|divine rank|Q11591025}} (see {{jalink|Susaki Shrine#Divine ranks|Q11556513|below}}). In the {{jalink|Engi|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) edition of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, it is recorded as “Kōshin Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto Shrine, Major, original name Susa-no-Kami” in Awa District, Awa Province, and was ranked as a major shrine. Susaki Shrine is one of the {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}} of this shrine, the other being Sunomiya Shrine, and disputes over which was the true Shikinaisha continued from the Edo period. In {{jalink|Eihō|Q1193881}} 1 ({{jalink|1081|Q19450}}), the deity’s rank reached the highest level of {{jalink|Senior First Rank|Q11123258}}. Later, in {{jalink|Kōan|Q389180}} 4 ({{jalink|1281|Q5537}}), it was awarded Second Order of Merit for contributions during the {{jalink|Mongol invasions of Japan|Q208156}}. In {{jalink|Jishō|Q1194671}} 4 ({{jalink|1180|Q19702}}), {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} fled by sea to Awa Province after his defeat at the {{jalink|Battle of Ishibashiyama|Q2888010}}. According to the {{jalink|Azuma Kagami|Q1042324}}, he visited Susaki Shrine to pray and donated shrine lands in gratitude for safe passage by sea{{jalink|Jirō Aida|Q11582311}}, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; reprinted by Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983). On {{jalink|Juei|Q598057}} 1 ({{jalink|1182|Q19708}}) August 11, {{jalink|Anzai Kageyasu|Q60990454}} was dispatched as an envoy to pray at Susaki Shrine for the safe childbirth of Yoritomo’s wife {{jalink|Hōjō Masako|Q463961|Masako}}. The Azuma Kagami also records an order in {{jalink|Yōwa|Q729609}} 1 ({{jalink|1181|Q19704}}) to stop disturbances by local officials in shrine lands of Susaki, addressed to Sunomiya Shrine priests, marking the earliest documentary evidence of relations between the two shrines. From ancient times, the shrine collected tolls from passing vessels, indicating control over maritime passage rights and sea checkpoints. This suggests that in the early and mid-medieval periods, Susaki Shrine held the greatest authority and power in Awa ProvinceTsuneari Takigawa, “The Satomi Clan of Bōsō and Maritime Transport in Edo Bay,” *Chiba Shigaku* No. 24, 1994. ==== Edo period ==== In {{jalink|Bunka|Q1373146}} 9 ({{jalink|1812|Q6933}}), the senior councilor {{jalink|Matsudaira Sadanobu|Q284038}} donated a plaque reading “Awa Province Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin.” Although this plaque served as the basis for identifying the shrine as Ichinomiya, the wording is ambiguous. The designation was later asserted in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}), though no historical text formally records it as Ichinomiya. The *History of Tateyama City* suggests this may have been a misunderstanding based on the existence of an Ichinomiya Road in Nishimisaki. Until the Edo period, the shrine was administered by the bettō temple Yōrō-ji, a situation that continued until the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} government issued the {{jalink|Shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|separation of Shinto and Buddhism}} decree in {{jalink|1868|Q7717}}. ==== Modern era ==== [[File:Susaki Shrine in 1926.jpg|250px|thumb|Around 1926 (Taishō 15)]] In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}}, the Ministry of Doctrine designated Sunomiya Shrine as the Shikinaisha, but reversed this decision the following year and designated Susaki Shrine instead, though the reasoning remains unclear. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine under the modern system. Located at a maritime chokepoint, the shrine maintained a custom until around {{jalink|1940|Q18633}}–{{jalink|1941|Q5231}} of collecting offerings from passing ships. In {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}, Mount Mitarai was designated a Chiba Prefectural Natural Monument as the “Susaki Shrine Natural Forest.” Today, the shrine is managed concurrently, with no resident priests. === Divine ranks === Chronology of divine ranks for “Amahiri-no-(To)-Me-no-Mikoto”: * {{jalink|Jōwa|Q1075605}} 9 ({{jalink|842|Q24730}}) Oct 2: Promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*Shoku Nihon Kōki*). * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 2 ({{jalink|852|Q30383}}) Aug 22: Promoted to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*). * {{jalink|Ten’an|Q1075611}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) Jan 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit to Senior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*). === Precincts === [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (Designated Cultural Property of Tateyama City)}}]] The main hall is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with copper roofing. Shrine tradition dates it to the {{jalink|Enpō|Q1145735}} era ({{jalink|1673|Q7617}}–{{jalink|1681|Q7645}}), though large-scale repairs occurred after the mid-Edo period. It is designated a Tangible Cultural Property of Tateyama City. The Zuijin Gate was built during the {{jalink|Hōei|Q1145262}} era, and 150 steps lead from behind it to the shrine buildings. Outside the beach torii stands a rounded stone called the “Sacred Stone,” said to be one of a pair dedicated from the Dragon Palace, the other enshrined at {{jalink|Awaguchi Shrine|Q59282598}} on the {{jalink|Miura Peninsula|Q536757}}. The Awaguchi stone represents “A-gyō,” while the Susaki stone represents “Un-gyō,” together guarding the entrance to Tokyo Bay{{refnest|group="Notes"|Information board.}}. Because the sea before the shrine is a key maritime route into Tokyo Bay, many votive plaques dedicated by ship captains for safe voyages were once seen before the war. After the shrine suffered major damage from the {{jalink|2019 Bōsō Peninsula Typhoon|Q67170176}} in {{jalink|2019|Q62609017}}, donations were received from various supporters, including the Tokyo talent agency {{jalink|Johnny & Associates|Q11728013|Johnny & Associates (then; now SMILE-UP.)}}, and used for reconstruction such as rebuilding torii of the Inari sub-shrine. A commemorative stone expressing gratitude for this support stands within the precincts. File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), haiden-2.jpg|Worship hall File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), keidaisha-1.jpg|Sub-shrine File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), keidaisha-2.jpg|Sub-shrine File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), shinmon.jpg|Zuijin Gate File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), shinseki.jpg|Sacred stone === Festivals === * Early February (Hatsuuma): Offering of the {{jalink|Susaki Minoko Odori|Q11556509|Susaki Dance}}. * Grand Festival ({{jalink|August 20|Q2824}}–{{jalink|August 21|Q2823}}): Portable shrine procession and Susaki Dance. === Cultural properties === === Chiba Prefecture–designated === * {{jalink|List of Chiba Prefecture Cultural Properties#Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11406175|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} ** {{jalink|Susaki Minoko Odori|Q11556509}} **: Consists of Miroku Dance and Kashima Dance. Designated June 9, {{jalink|1961|Q18051}}. * {{jalink|List of Chiba Prefecture Cultural Properties#Natural Monuments|Q11406175|Natural Monument}} ** Susaki Shrine Natural Forest **: A preserved natural forest on Mount Mitarai, designated September 29, {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}. === Tateyama City–designated === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Susaki Shrine Main Hall – designated February 21, {{jalink|1967|Q18277}}. ** Three documents titled “Susaki Daimyōjin Engi” – designated August 26, {{jalink|1970|Q18275}}. * Tangible Folk Cultural Property ** Sacred hair – said to be the hair of the enshrined deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto. === Branch shrines === * Underwater branch shrine (within Hasama Underwater Park): Established to pray for prevention of maritime accidents. * {{jalink|Shinagawa Shrine|Q11418419}} ({{jalink|Shinagawa Ward|Q233495}}, {{jalink|Tokyo Metropolis|Q1490}}): Transferred by Minamoto no Yoritomo for maritime safety. * Edo branch: Said to have been transferred by {{jalink|Ōta Dōkan|Q386144}} as the guardian deity of Edo. === Access === '''Address''' * Susaki 1697, Tateyama City, Chiba Prefecture '''Transportation''' * From {{jalink|Tateyama Station|Q553883}} ({{jalink|JR East|Q499071}}{{jalink|Uchibō Line|Q1142964}}) ** Take {{jalink|JR Bus|Q1010222}} (Susaki Line) to “Susaki Shrine” stop (about 30 minutes) '''Nearby''' * {{jalink|Sunozaki Lighthouse|Q1087248}} * Yajiri Well: About 700 m north of the shrine; associated with a legend of Minamoto no Yoritomo. === References === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} === Further reading === * Jirō Aida, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; reprinted 1983) * Fumio Kimizuka, “Susaki Shrine and Sunomiya Shrine” * *National Shrine Directory*, Vol. 1 * *Bōsō no Mori* * *Fundamental Research on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System* === Related articles === * {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}} – Ichinomiya of Awa Province. === External links === {{commonscat|Susaki-jinja}} * Susaki Shrine – Tateyama Tourism Association * Susaki Shrine – Minamibōsō Database {{DEFAULTSORT:Susaki Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:千葉県の神社]] [[Category:館山市の歴史]] [[Category:館山市の建築物]] [[Category:式内大社]] [[Category:安房国の式内社|大すさきしんしや]] [[Category:一宮|加]] [[Category:千葉県の旧県社]] [[Category:安房国|社1]] [[Category:忌部氏|社すさきしんしや]] [[Category:膳氏|社すさきしんしや]] [[Category:need translation]]

Worship hall |Location = {{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q80011}}{{jalink|Tateyama City|Q646755}} Susaki 1344 |Coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deity = {{jalink|Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Major) {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}}
(Traditionally) {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Main hall = Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}}, copper roofing |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = {{jalink|August 20|Q2824}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=34|lat_min=58|lat_sec=5|lon_deg=139|lon_min=45|lon_sec=29.6|label=Susaki Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), otorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Great torii gate}}]] [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), hamatorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Beach torii}}{{small|Facing the coast. The peak on the left is Mitarai-yama.}}]] '''Susaki Shrine''' (すさきじんじゃ / すのさきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Susaki, {{jalink|Tateyama City|Q646755}}, {{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q80011}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (major shrine) {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}}, and during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was regarded as the {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}}, it was classified as a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. The shrine is enshrined on the mid-slope of Mount Mitarai. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is as follows: * '''{{jalink|Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}}''' (あまのひりのめのみこと){{refnest|group="Notes"|The name and reading of the deity follow the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine.}} *: She is the consort of {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}}’s enshrined deity {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ama-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}, and was originally called “Susa no Kami.” === Variations in the deity’s name === The notation of the deity’s name differs in historical sources as follows: * Amahiri'''no'''me-no-Mikoto *: According to the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}. Works such as {{jalink|Suzuka no Muraji Tane|Q6536446}}’s {{jalink|Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708}}, the {{jalink|Ministry of Doctrine|Q4294653}}-compiled {{jalink|Tokusen Jinmei-chō|Q11571092}}, and the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine follow this notation. * Amahiri'''to'''me-no-Mikoto (あめのひりとめのみこと) *: According to sources older than the Jinmyōchō, such as the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}, {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}, and {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}. Documents such as “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki” and “Sunomiya Shrine Records” record the deity under this name. It has been suggested that this discrepancy arose because the Jinmyōchō mistakenly wrote the character “乃” instead of “刀” for the name originally recorded as Amahiritome-no-Mikoto in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}Kenichi Tanigawa (ed.), *Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 11 Kantō* (Hakusuisha, December 1984). According to the “Kanemaru Family Genealogical Record” ({{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 [{{jalink|1597|Q6790}}]), the Sunomiya Kō Shrine in Sunomiya Village, Awa District, later called Sunomiya Myōjin, was regarded as the inner sanctuary and the second shrine, while Susaki Myōjin on Mount Mitarai in Susaki Village was regarded as the worship hall and the first shrine. Based on this, it has been proposed that Susaki Shrine and {{jalink|Sunomiya Shrine|Q11556507}} formed a paired shrine complex enshrining the “Susa no Kami,” with Susaki Shrine as the first shrine and Sunomiya Shrine as the secondMedieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Research Group (ed.), *Fundamental Research on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System* (Iwata Shoin, February 2000). === History === === Founding === According to the {{jalink|Kogo Shūi|Q531555}} ({{jalink|Daido|Q1373191}} 2 [{{jalink|807|Q30016}}]), {{jalink|Amenotomi-no-Mikoto|Q24899653}}, acting on the orders of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, landed in Awa Province in search of fertile land and developed it. He later led part of the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229}} to the {{jalink|Bōsō Peninsula|Q1022115}} in pursuit of even richer land. According to the shrine tradition “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki,” compiled in {{jalink|Hōreki|Q1194397}} 3 ({{jalink|1753|Q7621}}), Susaki Shrine originated when Amenotomi-no-Mikoto enshrined a mirror belonging to his ancestral grandmother deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto on Mitarasu-yama (Mount Mitarai). Shrine tradition also recounts that in Yōrō 1 ({{jalink|717|Q23532}}), a great earthquake buried Kanegae Pond within the precincts, and a great serpent guarding a subterranean bell caused calamities. {{jalink|En no Gyōja|Q198946}} is said to have performed prayers for seven days and nights, received a divine oracle, and subdued the serpent, thereby removing the disaster. It is also said that En no Gyōja placed sacred stones for maritime safety on the shore in front of the beach torii and at Awaguchi Shrine in {{jalink|Yokosuka City|Q200578|Yokosuka}}{{refnest|group="Notes"|The paired sacred stone is enshrined as the sacred object of Awaguchi Shrine in Yoshii, Yokosuka City.}}. These legends indicate that Susaki Shrine was strongly influenced by {{jalink|Shugendō|Q1483875}} and {{jalink|Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088}} from early times. === Overview === ==== Medieval period ==== “Awa Province Amahiritome-no-Kami” appears frequently in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168}} and received successive promotions in {{jalink|divine rank|Q11591025}} (see {{jalink|Susaki Shrine#Divine ranks|Q11556513|below}}). In the {{jalink|Engi|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) edition of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, it is recorded as “Kōshin Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto Shrine, Major, original name Susa-no-Kami” in Awa District, Awa Province, and was ranked as a major shrine. Susaki Shrine is one of the {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}} of this shrine, the other being Sunomiya Shrine, and disputes over which was the true Shikinaisha continued from the Edo period. In {{jalink|Eihō|Q1193881}} 1 ({{jalink|1081|Q19450}}), the deity’s rank reached the highest level of {{jalink|Senior First Rank|Q11123258}}. Later, in {{jalink|Kōan|Q389180}} 4 ({{jalink|1281|Q5537}}), it was awarded Second Order of Merit for contributions during the {{jalink|Mongol invasions of Japan|Q208156}}. In {{jalink|Jishō|Q1194671}} 4 ({{jalink|1180|Q19702}}), {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} fled by sea to Awa Province after his defeat at the {{jalink|Battle of Ishibashiyama|Q2888010}}. According to the {{jalink|Azuma Kagami|Q1042324}}, he visited Susaki Shrine to pray and donated shrine lands in gratitude for safe passage by sea{{jalink|Jirō Aida|Q11582311}}, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; reprinted by Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983). On {{jalink|Juei|Q598057}} 1 ({{jalink|1182|Q19708}}) August 11, {{jalink|Anzai Kageyasu|Q60990454}} was dispatched as an envoy to pray at Susaki Shrine for the safe childbirth of Yoritomo’s wife {{jalink|Hōjō Masako|Q463961|Masako}}. The Azuma Kagami also records an order in {{jalink|Yōwa|Q729609}} 1 ({{jalink|1181|Q19704}}) to stop disturbances by local officials in shrine lands of Susaki, addressed to Sunomiya Shrine priests, marking the earliest documentary evidence of relations between the two shrines. From ancient times, the shrine collected tolls from passing vessels, indicating control over maritime passage rights and sea checkpoints. This suggests that in the early and mid-medieval periods, Susaki Shrine held the greatest authority and power in Awa ProvinceTsuneari Takigawa, “The Satomi Clan of Bōsō and Maritime Transport in Edo Bay,” *Chiba Shigaku* No. 24, 1994. ==== Edo period ==== In {{jalink|Bunka|Q1373146}} 9 ({{jalink|1812|Q6933}}), the senior councilor {{jalink|Matsudaira Sadanobu|Q284038}} donated a plaque reading “Awa Province Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin.” Although this plaque served as the basis for identifying the shrine as Ichinomiya, the wording is ambiguous. The designation was later asserted in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}), though no historical text formally records it as Ichinomiya. The *History of Tateyama City* suggests this may have been a misunderstanding based on the existence of an Ichinomiya Road in Nishimisaki. Until the Edo period, the shrine was administered by the bettō temple Yōrō-ji, a situation that continued until the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} government issued the {{jalink|Shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|separation of Shinto and Buddhism}} decree in {{jalink|1868|Q7717}}. ==== Modern era ==== [[File:Susaki Shrine in 1926.jpg|250px|thumb|Around 1926 (Taishō 15)]] In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}}, the Ministry of Doctrine designated Sunomiya Shrine as the Shikinaisha, but reversed this decision the following year and designated Susaki Shrine instead, though the reasoning remains unclear. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine under the modern system. Located at a maritime chokepoint, the shrine maintained a custom until around {{jalink|1940|Q18633}}–{{jalink|1941|Q5231}} of collecting offerings from passing ships. In {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}, Mount Mitarai was designated a Chiba Prefectural Natural Monument as the “Susaki Shrine Natural Forest.” Today, the shrine is managed concurrently, with no resident priests. === Divine ranks === Chronology of divine ranks for “Amahiri-no-(To)-Me-no-Mikoto”: * {{jalink|Jōwa|Q1075605}} 9 ({{jalink|842|Q24730}}) Oct 2: Promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*Shoku Nihon Kōki*). * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 2 ({{jalink|852|Q30383}}) Aug 22: Promoted to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*). * {{jalink|Ten’an|Q1075611}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) Jan 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit to Senior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*). === Precincts === [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (Designated Cultural Property of Tateyama City)}}]] The main hall is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with copper roofing. Shrine tradition dates it to the {{jalink|Enpō|Q1145735}} era ({{jalink|1673|Q7617}}–{{jalink|1681|Q7645}}), though large-scale repairs occurred after the mid-Edo period. It is designated a Tangible Cultural Property of Tateyama City. The Zuijin Gate was built during the {{jalink|Hōei|Q1145262}} era, and 150 steps lead from behind it to the shrine buildings. Outside the beach torii stands a rounded stone called the “Sacred Stone,” said to be one of a pair dedicated from the Dragon Palace, the other enshrined at {{jalink|Awaguchi Shrine|Q59282598}} on the {{jalink|Miura Peninsula|Q536757}}. The Awaguchi stone represents “A-gyō,” while the Susaki stone represents “Un-gyō,” together guarding the entrance to Tokyo Bay{{refnest|group="Notes"|Information board.}}. Because the sea before the shrine is a key maritime route into Tokyo Bay, many votive plaques dedicated by ship captains for safe voyages were once seen before the war. After the shrine suffered major damage from the {{jalink|2019 Bōsō Peninsula Typhoon|Q67170176}} in {{jalink|2019|Q62609017}}, donations were received from various supporters, including the Tokyo talent agency {{jalink|Johnny & Associates|Q11728013|Johnny & Associates (then; now SMILE-UP.)}}, and used for reconstruction such as rebuilding torii of the Inari sub-shrine. A commemorative stone expressing gratitude for this support stands within the precincts. File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), haiden-2.jpg|Worship hall File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), keidaisha-1.jpg|Sub-shrine File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), keidaisha-2.jpg|Sub-shrine File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), shinmon.jpg|Zuijin Gate File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), shinseki.jpg|Sacred stone === Festivals === * Early February (Hatsuuma): Offering of the {{jalink|Susaki Minoko Odori|Q11556509|Susaki Dance}}. * Grand Festival ({{jalink|August 20|Q2824}}–{{jalink|August 21|Q2823}}): Portable shrine procession and Susaki Dance. === Cultural properties === === Chiba Prefecture–designated === * {{jalink|List of Chiba Prefecture Cultural Properties#Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11406175|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} ** {{jalink|Susaki Minoko Odori|Q11556509}} **: Consists of Miroku Dance and Kashima Dance. Designated June 9, {{jalink|1961|Q18051}}. * {{jalink|List of Chiba Prefecture Cultural Properties#Natural Monuments|Q11406175|Natural Monument}} ** Susaki Shrine Natural Forest **: A preserved natural forest on Mount Mitarai, designated September 29, {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}. === Tateyama City–designated === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Susaki Shrine Main Hall – designated February 21, {{jalink|1967|Q18277}}. ** Three documents titled “Susaki Daimyōjin Engi” – designated August 26, {{jalink|1970|Q18275}}. * Tangible Folk Cultural Property ** Sacred hair – said to be the hair of the enshrined deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto. === Branch shrines === * Underwater branch shrine (within Hasama Underwater Park): Established to pray for prevention of maritime accidents. * {{jalink|Shinagawa Shrine|Q11418419}} ({{jalink|Shinagawa Ward|Q233495}}, {{jalink|Tokyo Metropolis|Q1490}}): Transferred by Minamoto no Yoritomo for maritime safety. * Edo branch: Said to have been transferred by {{jalink|Ōta Dōkan|Q386144}} as the guardian deity of Edo. === Access === '''Address''' * Susaki 1697, Tateyama City, Chiba Prefecture '''Transportation''' * From {{jalink|Tateyama Station|Q553883}} ({{jalink|JR East|Q499071}}{{jalink|Uchibō Line|Q1142964}}) ** Take {{jalink|JR Bus|Q1010222}} (Susaki Line) to “Susaki Shrine” stop (about 30 minutes) '''Nearby''' * {{jalink|Sunozaki Lighthouse|Q1087248}} * Yajiri Well: About 700 m north of the shrine; associated with a legend of Minamoto no Yoritomo. === References === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} === Further reading === * Jirō Aida, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; reprinted 1983) * Fumio Kimizuka, “Susaki Shrine and Sunomiya Shrine” * *National Shrine Directory*, Vol. 1 * *Bōsō no Mori* * *Fundamental Research on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System* === Related articles === * {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}} – Ichinomiya of Awa Province. === External links === {{commonscat|Susaki-jinja}} * Susaki Shrine – Tateyama Tourism Association * Susaki Shrine – Minamibōsō Database {{DEFAULTSORT:Susaki Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:千葉県の神社]] [[Category:館山市の歴史]] [[Category:館山市の建築物]] [[Category:式内大社]] [[Category:安房国の式内社|大すさきしんしや]] [[Category:一宮|加]] [[Category:千葉県の旧県社]] [[Category:安房国|社1]] [[Category:忌部氏|社すさきしんしや]] [[Category:膳氏|社すさきしんしや]]

[[SIMPLE:Susaki Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11556513}} == Japanese content == {{Otheruses}} {{Shrine |Name = Susaki Shrine |Image = [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), haiden-1.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q80011}}{{jalink|Tateyama City|Q646755}} Susaki 1344 |Coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deity = {{jalink|Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Major) {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}}
(Traditionally) {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Main hall = Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}}, copper roofing |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = {{jalink|August 20|Q2824}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=34|lat_min=58|lat_sec=5|lon_deg=139|lon_min=45|lon_sec=29.6|label=Susaki Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), otorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Great torii gate}}]] [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), hamatorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Beach torii}}{{small|Facing the coast. The peak on the left is Mitarai-yama.}}]] '''Susaki Shrine''' (すさきじんじゃ / すのさきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Susaki, {{jalink|Tateyama City|Q646755}}, {{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q80011}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (major shrine) {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}}, and during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was regarded as the {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}}, it was classified as a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. The shrine is enshrined on the mid-slope of Mount Mitarai. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is as follows: * '''{{jalink|Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}}''' (あまのひりのめのみこと){{refnest|group="Notes"|The name and reading of the deity follow the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine.}} *: She is the consort of {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}}’s enshrined deity {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ama-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}, and was originally called “Susa no Kami.” === Variations in the deity’s name === The notation of the deity’s name differs in historical sources as follows: * Amahiri'''no'''me-no-Mikoto *: According to the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}. Works such as {{jalink|Suzuka no Muraji Tane|Q6536446}}’s {{jalink|Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708}}, the {{jalink|Ministry of Doctrine|Q4294653}}-compiled {{jalink|Tokusen Jinmei-chō|Q11571092}}, and the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine follow this notation. * Amahiri'''to'''me-no-Mikoto (あめのひりとめのみこと) *: According to sources older than the Jinmyōchō, such as the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}, {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}, and {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}. Documents such as “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki” and “Sunomiya Shrine Records” record the deity under this name. It has been suggested that this discrepancy arose because the Jinmyōchō mistakenly wrote the character “乃” instead of “刀” for the name originally recorded as Amahiritome-no-Mikoto in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}Kenichi Tanigawa (ed.), *Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 11 Kantō* (Hakusuisha, December 1984). According to the “Kanemaru Family Genealogical Record” ({{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 [{{jalink|1597|Q6790}}]), the Sunomiya Kō Shrine in Sunomiya Village, Awa District, later called Sunomiya Myōjin, was regarded as the inner sanctuary and the second shrine, while Susaki Myōjin on Mount Mitarai in Susaki Village was regarded as the worship hall and the first shrine. Based on this, it has been proposed that Susaki Shrine and {{jalink|Sunomiya Shrine|Q11556507}} formed a paired shrine complex enshrining the “Susa no Kami,” with Susaki Shrine as the first shrine and Sunomiya Shrine as the secondMedieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Research Group (ed.), *Fundamental Research on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System* (Iwata Shoin, February 2000). === History === === Founding === According to the {{jalink|Kogo Shūi|Q531555}} ({{jalink|Daido|Q1373191}} 2 [{{jalink|807|Q30016}}]), {{jalink|Amenotomi-no-Mikoto|Q24899653}}, acting on the orders of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, landed in Awa Province in search of fertile land and developed it. He later led part of the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229}} to the {{jalink|Bōsō Peninsula|Q1022115}} in pursuit of even richer land. According to the shrine tradition “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki,” compiled in {{jalink|Hōreki|Q1194397}} 3 ({{jalink|1753|Q7621}}), Susaki Shrine originated when Amenotomi-no-Mikoto enshrined a mirror belonging to his ancestral grandmother deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto on Mitarasu-yama (Mount Mitarai). Shrine tradition also recounts that in Yōrō 1 ({{jalink|717|Q23532}}), a great earthquake buried Kanegae Pond within the precincts, and a great serpent guarding a subterranean bell caused calamities. {{jalink|En no Gyōja|Q198946}} is said to have performed prayers for seven days and nights, received a divine oracle, and subdued the serpent, thereby removing the disaster. It is also said that En no Gyōja placed sacred stones for maritime safety on the shore in front of the beach torii and at Awaguchi Shrine in {{jalink|Yokosuka City|Q200578|Yokosuka}}{{refnest|group="Notes"|The paired sacred stone is enshrined as the sacred object of Awaguchi Shrine in Yoshii, Yokosuka City.}}. These legends indicate that Susaki Shrine was strongly influenced by {{jalink|Shugendō|Q1483875}} and {{jalink|Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088}} from early times. === Overview === ==== Medieval period ==== “Awa Province Amahiritome-no-Kami” appears frequently in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168}} and received successive promotions in {{jalink|divine rank|Q11591025}} (see {{jalink|Susaki Shrine#Divine ranks|Q11556513|below}}). In the {{jalink|Engi|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) edition of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, it is recorded as “Kōshin Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto Shrine, Major, original name Susa-no-Kami” in Awa District, Awa Province, and was ranked as a major shrine. Susaki Shrine is one of the {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}} of this shrine, the other being Sunomiya Shrine, and disputes over which was the true Shikinaisha continued from the Edo period. In {{jalink|Eihō|Q1193881}} 1 ({{jalink|1081|Q19450}}), the deity’s rank reached the highest level of {{jalink|Senior First Rank|Q11123258}}. Later, in {{jalink|Kōan|Q389180}} 4 ({{jalink|1281|Q5537}}), it was awarded Second Order of Merit for contributions during the {{jalink|Mongol invasions of Japan|Q208156}}. In {{jalink|Jishō|Q1194671}} 4 ({{jalink|1180|Q19702}}), {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} fled by sea to Awa Province after his defeat at the {{jalink|Battle of Ishibashiyama|Q2888010}}. According to the {{jalink|Azuma Kagami|Q1042324}}, he visited Susaki Shrine to pray and donated shrine lands in gratitude for safe passage by sea{{jalink|Jirō Aida|Q11582311}}, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; reprinted by Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983). On {{jalink|Juei|Q598057}} 1 ({{jalink|1182|Q19708}}) August 11, {{jalink|Anzai Kageyasu|Q60990454}} was dispatched as an envoy to pray at Susaki Shrine for the safe childbirth of Yoritomo’s wife {{jalink|Hōjō Masako|Q463961|Masako}}. The Azuma Kagami also records an order in {{jalink|Yōwa|Q729609}} 1 ({{jalink|1181|Q19704}}) to stop disturbances by local officials in shrine lands of Susaki, addressed to Sunomiya Shrine priests, marking the earliest documentary evidence of relations between the two shrines. From ancient times, the shrine collected tolls from passing vessels, indicating control over maritime passage rights and sea checkpoints. This suggests that in the early and mid-medieval periods, Susaki Shrine held the greatest authority and power in Awa ProvinceTsuneari Takigawa, “The Satomi Clan of Bōsō and Maritime Transport in Edo Bay,” *Chiba Shigaku* No. 24, 1994. ==== Edo period ==== In {{jalink|Bunka|Q1373146}} 9 ({{jalink|1812|Q6933}}), the senior councilor {{jalink|Matsudaira Sadanobu|Q284038}} donated a plaque reading “Awa Province Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin.” Although this plaque served as the basis for identifying the shrine as Ichinomiya, the wording is ambiguous. The designation was later asserted in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}), though no historical text formally records it as Ichinomiya. The *History of Tateyama City* suggests this may have been a misunderstanding based on the existence of an Ichinomiya Road in Nishimisaki. Until the Edo period, the shrine was administered by the bettō temple Yōrō-ji, a situation that continued until the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} government issued the {{jalink|Shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|separation of Shinto and Buddhism}} decree in {{jalink|1868|Q7717}}. ==== Modern era ==== [[File:Susaki Shrine in 1926.jpg|250px|thumb|Around 1926 (Taishō 15)]] In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}}, the Ministry of Doctrine designated Sunomiya Shrine as the Shikinaisha, but reversed this decision the following year and designated Susaki Shrine instead, though the reasoning remains unclear. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine under the modern system. Located at a maritime chokepoint, the shrine maintained a custom until around {{jalink|1940|Q18633}}–{{jalink|1941|Q5231}} of collecting offerings from passing ships. In {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}, Mount Mitarai was designated a Chiba Prefectural Natural Monument as the “Susaki Shrine Natural Forest.” Today, the shrine is managed concurrently, with no resident priests. === Divine ranks === Chronology of divine ranks for “Amahiri-no-(To)-Me-no-Mikoto”: * {{jalink|Jōwa|Q1075605}} 9 ({{jalink|842|Q24730}}) Oct 2: Promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*Shoku Nihon Kōki*). * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 2 ({{jalink|852|Q30383}}) Aug 22: Promoted to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*). * {{jalink|Ten’an|Q1075611}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) Jan 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit to Senior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*). === Precincts === [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (Designated Cultural Property of Tateyama City)}}]] The main hall is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with copper roofing. Shrine tradition dates it to the {{jalink|Enpō|Q1145735}} era ({{jalink|1673|Q7617}}–{{jalink|1681|Q7645}}), though large-scale repairs occurred after the mid-Edo period. It is designated a Tangible Cultural Property of Tateyama City. The Zuijin Gate was built during the {{jalink|Hōei|Q1145262}} era, and 150 steps lead from behind it to the shrine buildings. Outside the beach torii stands a rounded stone called the “Sacred Stone,” said to be one of a pair dedicated from the Dragon Palace, the other enshrined at {{jalink|Awaguchi Shrine|Q59282598}} on the {{jalink|Miura Peninsula|Q536757}}. The Awaguchi stone represents “A-gyō,” while the Susaki stone represents “Un-gyō,” together guarding the entrance to Tokyo Bay{{refnest|group="Notes"|Information board.}}. Because the sea before the shrine is a key maritime route into Tokyo Bay, many votive plaques dedicated by ship captains for safe voyages were once seen before the war. After the shrine suffered major damage from the {{jalink|2019 Bōsō Peninsula Typhoon|Q67170176}} in {{jalink|2019|Q62609017}}, donations were received from various supporters, including the Tokyo talent agency {{jalink|Johnny & Associates|Q11728013|Johnny & Associates (then; now SMILE-UP.)}}, and used for reconstruction such as rebuilding torii of the Inari sub-shrine. A commemorative stone expressing gratitude for this support stands within the precincts. File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), haiden-2.jpg|Worship hall File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), keidaisha-1.jpg|Sub-shrine File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), keidaisha-2.jpg|Sub-shrine File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), shinmon.jpg|Zuijin Gate File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), shinseki.jpg|Sacred stone === Festivals === * Early February (Hatsuuma): Offering of the {{jalink|Susaki Minoko Odori|Q11556509|Susaki Dance}}. * Grand Festival ({{jalink|August 20|Q2824}}–{{jalink|August 21|Q2823}}): Portable shrine procession and Susaki Dance. === Cultural properties === === Chiba Prefecture–designated === * {{jalink|List of Chiba Prefecture Cultural Properties#Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11406175|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} ** {{jalink|Susaki Minoko Odori|Q11556509}} **: Consists of Miroku Dance and Kashima Dance. Designated June 9, {{jalink|1961|Q18051}}. * {{jalink|List of Chiba Prefecture Cultural Properties#Natural Monuments|Q11406175|Natural Monument}} ** Susaki Shrine Natural Forest **: A preserved natural forest on Mount Mitarai, designated September 29, {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}. === Tateyama City–designated === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Susaki Shrine Main Hall – designated February 21, {{jalink|1967|Q18277}}. ** Three documents titled “Susaki Daimyōjin Engi” – designated August 26, {{jalink|1970|Q18275}}. * Tangible Folk Cultural Property ** Sacred hair – said to be the hair of the enshrined deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto. === Branch shrines === * Underwater branch shrine (within Hasama Underwater Park): Established to pray for prevention of maritime accidents. * {{jalink|Shinagawa Shrine|Q11418419}} ({{jalink|Shinagawa Ward|Q233495}}, {{jalink|Tokyo Metropolis|Q1490}}): Transferred by Minamoto no Yoritomo for maritime safety. * Edo branch: Said to have been transferred by {{jalink|Ōta Dōkan|Q386144}} as the guardian deity of Edo. === Access === '''Address''' * Susaki 1697, Tateyama City, Chiba Prefecture '''Transportation''' * From {{jalink|Tateyama Station|Q553883}} ({{jalink|JR East|Q499071}}{{jalink|Uchibō Line|Q1142964}}) ** Take {{jalink|JR Bus|Q1010222}} (Susaki Line) to “Susaki Shrine” stop (about 30 minutes) '''Nearby''' * {{jalink|Sunozaki Lighthouse|Q1087248}} * Yajiri Well: About 700 m north of the shrine; associated with a legend of Minamoto no Yoritomo. === References === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} === Further reading === * Jirō Aida, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; reprinted 1983) * Fumio Kimizuka, “Susaki Shrine and Sunomiya Shrine” * *National Shrine Directory*, Vol. 1 * *Bōsō no Mori* * *Fundamental Research on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System* === Related articles === * {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}} – Ichinomiya of Awa Province. === External links === {{commonscat|Susaki-jinja}} * Susaki Shrine – Tateyama Tourism Association * Susaki Shrine – Minamibōsō Database {{DEFAULTSORT:Susaki Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Susaki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:千葉県の神社]] [[Category:館山市の歴史]] [[Category:館山市の建築物]] [[Category:式内大社]] [[Category:安房国の式内社|大すさきしんしや]] [[Category:一宮|加]] [[Category:千葉県の旧県社]] [[Category:安房国|社1]] [[Category:忌部氏|社すさきしんしや]] [[Category:膳氏|社すさきしんしや]]

(Traditionally) {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Main hall = Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}}, copper roofing |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = {{jalink|August 20|Q2824}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=34|lat_min=58|lat_sec=5|lon_deg=139|lon_min=45|lon_sec=29.6|label=Susaki Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), otorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Great torii gate}}]] [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), hamatorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Beach torii}}{{small|Facing the coast. The peak on the left is Mitarai-yama.}}]] '''Susaki Shrine''' (すさきじんじゃ / すのさきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Susaki, {{jalink|Tateyama City|Q646755}}, {{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q80011}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (major shrine) {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}}, and during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was regarded as the {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}}, it was classified as a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. The shrine is enshrined on the mid-slope of Mount Mitarai. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is as follows: * '''{{jalink|Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}}''' (あまのひりのめのみこと){{refnest|group="Notes"|The name and reading of the deity follow the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine.}} *: She is the consort of {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}}’s enshrined deity {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ama-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}, and was originally called “Susa no Kami.” === Variations in the deity’s name === The notation of the deity’s name differs in historical sources as follows: * Amahiri'''no'''me-no-Mikoto *: According to the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}. Works such as {{jalink|Suzuka no Muraji Tane|Q6536446}}’s {{jalink|Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708}}, the {{jalink|Ministry of Doctrine|Q4294653}}-compiled {{jalink|Tokusen Jinmei-chō|Q11571092}}, and the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine follow this notation. * Amahiri'''to'''me-no-Mikoto (あめのひりとめのみこと) *: According to sources older than the Jinmyōchō, such as the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}, {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}, and {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}. Documents such as “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki” and “Sunomiya Shrine Records” record the deity under this name. It has been suggested that this discrepancy arose because the Jinmyōchō mistakenly wrote the character “乃” instead of “刀” for the name originally recorded as Amahiritome-no-Mikoto in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}Kenichi Tanigawa (ed.), *Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 11 Kantō* (Hakusuisha, December 1984). According to the “Kanemaru Family Genealogical Record” ({{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 [{{jalink|1597|Q6790}}]), the Sunomiya Kō Shrine in Sunomiya Village, Awa District, later called Sunomiya Myōjin, was regarded as the inner sanctuary and the second shrine, while Susaki Myōjin on Mount Mitarai in Susaki Village was regarded as the worship hall and the first shrine. Based on this, it has been proposed that Susaki Shrine and {{jalink|Sunomiya Shrine|Q11556507}} formed a paired shrine complex enshrining the “Susa no Kami,” with Susaki Shrine as the first shrine and Sunomiya Shrine as the secondMedieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Research Group (ed.), *Fundamental Research on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System* (Iwata Shoin, February 2000). === History === === Founding === According to the {{jalink|Kogo Shūi|Q531555}} ({{jalink|Daido|Q1373191}} 2 [{{jalink|807|Q30016}}]), {{jalink|Amenotomi-no-Mikoto|Q24899653}}, acting on the orders of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, landed in Awa Province in search of fertile land and developed it. He later led part of the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229}} to the {{jalink|Bōsō Peninsula|Q1022115}} in pursuit of even richer land. According to the shrine tradition “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki,” compiled in {{jalink|Hōreki|Q1194397}} 3 ({{jalink|1753|Q7621}}), Susaki Shrine originated when Amenotomi-no-Mikoto enshrined a mirror belonging to his ancestral grandmother deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto on Mitarasu-yama (Mount Mitarai). Shrine tradition also recounts that in Yōrō 1 ({{jalink|717|Q23532}}), a great earthquake buried Kanegae Pond within the precincts, and a great serpent guarding a subterranean bell caused calamities. {{jalink|En no Gyōja|Q198946}} is said to have performed prayers for seven days and nights, received a divine oracle, and subdued the serpent, thereby removing the disaster. It is also said that En no Gyōja placed sacred stones for maritime safety on the shore in front of the beach torii and at Awaguchi Shrine in {{jalink|Yokosuka City|Q200578|Yokosuka}}{{refnest|group="Notes"|The paired sacred stone is enshrined as the sacred object of Awaguchi Shrine in Yoshii, Yokosuka City.}}. These legends indicate that Susaki Shrine was strongly influenced by {{jalink|Shugendō|Q1483875}} and {{jalink|Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088}} from early times. === Overview === ==== Medieval period ==== “Awa Province Amahiritome-no-Kami” appears frequently in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168}} and received successive promotions in {{jalink|divine rank|Q11591025}} (see {{jalink|Susaki Shrine#Divine ranks|Q11556513|below}}). In the {{jalink|Engi|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) edition of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, it is recorded as “Kōshin Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto Shrine, Major, original name Susa-no-Kami” in Awa District, Awa Province, and was ranked as a major shrine. Susaki Shrine is one of the {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}} of this shrine, the other being Sunomiya Shrine, and disputes over which was the true Shikinaisha continued from the Edo period. In {{jalink|Eihō|Q1193881}} 1 ({{jalink|1081|Q19450}}), the deity’s rank reached the highest level of {{jalink|Senior First Rank|Q11123258}}. Later, in {{jalink|Kōan|Q389180}} 4 ({{jalink|1281|Q5537}}), it was awarded Second Order of Merit for contributions during the {{jalink|Mongol invasions of Japan|Q208156}}. In {{jalink|Jishō|Q1194671}} 4 ({{jalink|1180|Q19702}}), {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} fled by sea to Awa Province after his defeat at the {{jalink|Battle of Ishibashiyama|Q2888010}}. According to the {{jalink|Azuma Kagami|Q1042324}}, he visited Susaki Shrine to pray and donated shrine lands in gratitude for safe passage by sea{{jalink|Jirō Aida|Q11582311}}, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; reprinted by Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983). On {{jalink|Juei|Q598057}} 1 ({{jalink|1182|Q19708}}) August 11, {{jalink|Anzai Kageyasu|Q60990454}} was dispatched as an envoy to pray at Susaki Shrine for the safe childbirth of Yoritomo’s wife {{jalink|Hōjō Masako|Q463961|Masako}}. The Azuma Kagami also records an order in {{jalink|Yōwa|Q729609}} 1 ({{jalink|1181|Q19704}}) to stop disturbances by local officials in shrine lands of Susaki, addressed to Sunomiya Shrine priests, marking the earliest documentary evidence of relations between the two shrines. From ancient times, the shrine collected tolls from passing vessels, indicating control over maritime passage rights and sea checkpoints. This suggests that in the early and mid-medieval periods, Susaki Shrine held the greatest authority and power in Awa ProvinceTsuneari Takigawa, “The Satomi Clan of Bōsō and Maritime Transport in Edo Bay,” *Chiba Shigaku* No. 24, 1994. ==== Edo period ==== In {{jalink|Bunka|Q1373146}} 9 ({{jalink|1812|Q6933}}), the senior councilor {{jalink|Matsudaira Sadanobu|Q284038}} donated a plaque reading “Awa Province Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin.” Although this plaque served as the basis for identifying the shrine as Ichinomiya, the wording is ambiguous. The designation was later asserted in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}), though no historical text formally records it as Ichinomiya. The *History of Tateyama City* suggests this may have been a misunderstanding based on the existence of an Ichinomiya Road in Nishimisaki. Until the Edo period, the shrine was administered by the bettō temple Yōrō-ji, a situation that continued until the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} government issued the {{jalink|Shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|separation of Shinto and Buddhism}} decree in {{jalink|1868|Q7717}}. ==== Modern era ==== [[File:Susaki Shrine in 1926.jpg|250px|thumb|Around 1926 (Taishō 15)]] In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}}, the Ministry of Doctrine designated Sunomiya Shrine as the Shikinaisha, but reversed this decision the following year and designated Susaki Shrine instead, though the reasoning remains unclear. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine under the modern system. Located at a maritime chokepoint, the shrine maintained a custom until around {{jalink|1940|Q18633}}–{{jalink|1941|Q5231}} of collecting offerings from passing ships. In {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}, Mount Mitarai was designated a Chiba Prefectural Natural Monument as the “Susaki Shrine Natural Forest.” Today, the shrine is managed concurrently, with no resident priests. === Divine ranks === Chronology of divine ranks for “Amahiri-no-(To)-Me-no-Mikoto”: * {{jalink|Jōwa|Q1075605}} 9 ({{jalink|842|Q24730}}) Oct 2: Promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*Shoku Nihon Kōki*). * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 2 ({{jalink|852|Q30383}}) Aug 22: Promoted to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*). * {{jalink|Ten’an|Q1075611}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) Jan 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit to Senior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*). === Precincts === [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (Designated Cultural Property of Tateyama City)}}]] The main hall is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with copper roofing. Shrine tradition dates it to the {{jalink|Enpō|Q1145735}} era ({{jalink|1673|Q7617}}–{{jalink|1681|Q7645}}), though large-scale repairs occurred after the mid-Edo period. It is designated a Tangible Cultural Property of Tateyama City. The Zuijin Gate was built during the {{jalink|Hōei|Q1145262}} era, and 150 steps lead from behind it to the shrine buildings. Outside the beach torii stands a rounded stone called the “Sacred Stone,” said to be one of a pair dedicated from the Dragon Palace, the other enshrined at {{jalink|Awaguchi Shrine|Q59282598}} on the {{jalink|Miura Peninsula|Q536757}}. The Awaguchi stone represents “A-gyō,” while the Susaki stone represents “Un-gyō,” together guarding the entrance to Tokyo Bay{{refnest|group="Notes"|Information board.}}. Because the sea before the shrine is a key maritime route into Tokyo Bay, many votive plaques dedicated by ship captains for safe voyages were once seen before the war. After the shrine suffered major damage from the {{jalink|2019 Bōsō Peninsula Typhoon|Q67170176}} in {{jalink|2019|Q62609017}}, donations were received from various supporters, including the Tokyo talent agency {{jalink|Johnny & Associates|Q11728013|Johnny & Associates (then; now SMILE-UP.)}}, and used for reconstruction such as rebuilding torii of the Inari sub-shrine. A commemorative stone expressing gratitude for this support stands within the precincts. Designated June 9, {{jalink|1961|Q18051}}. * {{jalink|List of Chiba Prefecture Cultural Properties#Natural Monuments|Q11406175|Natural Monument}} ** Susaki Shrine Natural Forest **: A preserved natural forest on Mount Mitarai, designated September 29, {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}. === Tateyama City–designated === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Susaki Shrine Main Hall – designated February 21, {{jalink|1967|Q18277}}. ** Three documents titled “Susaki Daimyōjin Engi” – designated August 26, {{jalink|1970|Q18275}}. * Tangible Folk Cultural Property ** Sacred hair – said to be the hair of the enshrined deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto. === Branch shrines === * Underwater branch shrine (within Hasama Underwater Park): Established to pray for prevention of maritime accidents. * {{jalink|Shinagawa Shrine|Q11418419}} ({{jalink|Shinagawa Ward|Q233495}}, {{jalink|Tokyo Metropolis|Q1490}}): Transferred by Minamoto no Yoritomo for maritime safety. * Edo branch: Said to have been transferred by {{jalink|Ōta Dōkan|Q386144}} as the guardian deity of Edo. === Access === '''Address''' * Susaki 1697, Tateyama City, Chiba Prefecture '''Transportation''' * From {{jalink|Tateyama Station|Q553883}} ({{jalink|JR East|Q499071}}{{jalink|Uchibō Line|Q1142964}}) ** Take {{jalink|JR Bus|Q1010222}} (Susaki Line) to “Susaki Shrine” stop (about 30 minutes) '''Nearby''' * {{jalink|Sunozaki Lighthouse|Q1087248}} * Yajiri Well: About 700 m north of the shrine; associated with a legend of Minamoto no Yoritomo. === References === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} === Further reading === * Jirō Aida, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; reprinted 1983) * Fumio Kimizuka, “Susaki Shrine and Sunomiya Shrine” * *National Shrine Directory*, Vol. 1 * *Bōsō no Mori* * *Fundamental Research on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System* === Related articles === * {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}} – Ichinomiya of Awa Province. === External links === {{commonscat|Susaki-jinja}} * Susaki Shrine – Tateyama Tourism Association * Susaki Shrine – Minamibōsō Database {{DEFAULTSORT:Susaki Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Susaki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Susaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:千葉県の神社]] [[Category:館山市の歴史]] [[Category:館山市の建築物]] [[Category:式内大社]] [[Category:安房国の式内社|大すさきしんしや]] [[Category:一宮|加]] [[Category:千葉県の旧県社]] [[Category:安房国|社1]] [[Category:忌部氏|社すさきしんしや]] [[Category:膳氏|社すさきしんしや]]

Worship hall |Location = {{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q80011}}{{jalink|Tateyama City|Q646755}} Susaki 1344 |Coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deity = {{jalink|Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Major) {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}}
(Traditionally) {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Main hall = Three-bay shrine, {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}}, copper roofing |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = {{jalink|August 20|Q2824}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=34|lat_min=58|lat_sec=5|lon_deg=139|lon_min=45|lon_sec=29.6|label=Susaki Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), otorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Great torii gate}}]] [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), hamatorii.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{center|Beach torii}}{{small|Facing the coast. The peak on the left is Mitarai-yama.}}]] '''Susaki Shrine''' (すさきじんじゃ / すのさきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Susaki, {{jalink|Tateyama City|Q646755}}, {{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q80011}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (major shrine) {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}}, and during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was regarded as the {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044}}. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}}, it was classified as a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. The shrine is enshrined on the mid-slope of Mount Mitarai. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is as follows: * '''{{jalink|Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}}''' (あまのひりのめのみこと){{refnest|group="Notes"|The name and reading of the deity follow the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine.}} *: She is the consort of {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}}’s enshrined deity {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ama-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}}, and was originally called “Susa no Kami.” === Variations in the deity’s name === The notation of the deity’s name differs in historical sources as follows: * Amahiri'''no'''me-no-Mikoto *: According to the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}. Works such as {{jalink|Suzuka no Muraji Tane|Q6536446}}’s {{jalink|Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708}}, the {{jalink|Ministry of Doctrine|Q4294653}}-compiled {{jalink|Tokusen Jinmei-chō|Q11571092}}, and the “Sanpai no Shiori” distributed by Susaki Shrine follow this notation. * Amahiri'''to'''me-no-Mikoto (あめのひりとめのみこと) *: According to sources older than the Jinmyōchō, such as the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}, {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}, and {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}. Documents such as “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki” and “Sunomiya Shrine Records” record the deity under this name. It has been suggested that this discrepancy arose because the Jinmyōchō mistakenly wrote the character “乃” instead of “刀” for the name originally recorded as Amahiritome-no-Mikoto in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}Kenichi Tanigawa (ed.), *Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 11 Kantō* (Hakusuisha, December 1984). According to the “Kanemaru Family Genealogical Record” ({{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 [{{jalink|1597|Q6790}}]), the Sunomiya Kō Shrine in Sunomiya Village, Awa District, later called Sunomiya Myōjin, was regarded as the inner sanctuary and the second shrine, while Susaki Myōjin on Mount Mitarai in Susaki Village was regarded as the worship hall and the first shrine. Based on this, it has been proposed that Susaki Shrine and {{jalink|Sunomiya Shrine|Q11556507}} formed a paired shrine complex enshrining the “Susa no Kami,” with Susaki Shrine as the first shrine and Sunomiya Shrine as the secondMedieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Research Group (ed.), *Fundamental Research on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System* (Iwata Shoin, February 2000). === History === === Founding === According to the {{jalink|Kogo Shūi|Q531555}} ({{jalink|Daido|Q1373191}} 2 [{{jalink|807|Q30016}}]), {{jalink|Amenotomi-no-Mikoto|Q24899653}}, acting on the orders of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, landed in Awa Province in search of fertile land and developed it. He later led part of the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229}} to the {{jalink|Bōsō Peninsula|Q1022115}} in pursuit of even richer land. According to the shrine tradition “Susaki Daimyōjin Yui-sho Kyūki,” compiled in {{jalink|Hōreki|Q1194397}} 3 ({{jalink|1753|Q7621}}), Susaki Shrine originated when Amenotomi-no-Mikoto enshrined a mirror belonging to his ancestral grandmother deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto on Mitarasu-yama (Mount Mitarai). Shrine tradition also recounts that in Yōrō 1 ({{jalink|717|Q23532}}), a great earthquake buried Kanegae Pond within the precincts, and a great serpent guarding a subterranean bell caused calamities. {{jalink|En no Gyōja|Q198946}} is said to have performed prayers for seven days and nights, received a divine oracle, and subdued the serpent, thereby removing the disaster. It is also said that En no Gyōja placed sacred stones for maritime safety on the shore in front of the beach torii and at Awaguchi Shrine in {{jalink|Yokosuka City|Q200578|Yokosuka}}{{refnest|group="Notes"|The paired sacred stone is enshrined as the sacred object of Awaguchi Shrine in Yoshii, Yokosuka City.}}. These legends indicate that Susaki Shrine was strongly influenced by {{jalink|Shugendō|Q1483875}} and {{jalink|Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088}} from early times. === Overview === ==== Medieval period ==== “Awa Province Amahiritome-no-Kami” appears frequently in the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168}} and received successive promotions in {{jalink|divine rank|Q11591025}} (see {{jalink|Susaki Shrine#Divine ranks|Q11556513|below}}). In the {{jalink|Engi|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) edition of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, it is recorded as “Kōshin Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto Shrine, Major, original name Susa-no-Kami” in Awa District, Awa Province, and was ranked as a major shrine. Susaki Shrine is one of the {{jalink|Ronsha|Q135022904}} of this shrine, the other being Sunomiya Shrine, and disputes over which was the true Shikinaisha continued from the Edo period. In {{jalink|Eihō|Q1193881}} 1 ({{jalink|1081|Q19450}}), the deity’s rank reached the highest level of {{jalink|Senior First Rank|Q11123258}}. Later, in {{jalink|Kōan|Q389180}} 4 ({{jalink|1281|Q5537}}), it was awarded Second Order of Merit for contributions during the {{jalink|Mongol invasions of Japan|Q208156}}. In {{jalink|Jishō|Q1194671}} 4 ({{jalink|1180|Q19702}}), {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} fled by sea to Awa Province after his defeat at the {{jalink|Battle of Ishibashiyama|Q2888010}}. According to the {{jalink|Azuma Kagami|Q1042324}}, he visited Susaki Shrine to pray and donated shrine lands in gratitude for safe passage by sea{{jalink|Jirō Aida|Q11582311}}, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; reprinted by Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983). On {{jalink|Juei|Q598057}} 1 ({{jalink|1182|Q19708}}) August 11, {{jalink|Anzai Kageyasu|Q60990454}} was dispatched as an envoy to pray at Susaki Shrine for the safe childbirth of Yoritomo’s wife {{jalink|Hōjō Masako|Q463961|Masako}}. The Azuma Kagami also records an order in {{jalink|Yōwa|Q729609}} 1 ({{jalink|1181|Q19704}}) to stop disturbances by local officials in shrine lands of Susaki, addressed to Sunomiya Shrine priests, marking the earliest documentary evidence of relations between the two shrines. From ancient times, the shrine collected tolls from passing vessels, indicating control over maritime passage rights and sea checkpoints. This suggests that in the early and mid-medieval periods, Susaki Shrine held the greatest authority and power in Awa ProvinceTsuneari Takigawa, “The Satomi Clan of Bōsō and Maritime Transport in Edo Bay,” *Chiba Shigaku* No. 24, 1994. ==== Edo period ==== In {{jalink|Bunka|Q1373146}} 9 ({{jalink|1812|Q6933}}), the senior councilor {{jalink|Matsudaira Sadanobu|Q284038}} donated a plaque reading “Awa Province Ichinomiya Susaki Daimyōjin.” Although this plaque served as the basis for identifying the shrine as Ichinomiya, the wording is ambiguous. The designation was later asserted in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}), though no historical text formally records it as Ichinomiya. The *History of Tateyama City* suggests this may have been a misunderstanding based on the existence of an Ichinomiya Road in Nishimisaki. Until the Edo period, the shrine was administered by the bettō temple Yōrō-ji, a situation that continued until the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} government issued the {{jalink|Shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|separation of Shinto and Buddhism}} decree in {{jalink|1868|Q7717}}. ==== Modern era ==== [[File:Susaki Shrine in 1926.jpg|250px|thumb|Around 1926 (Taishō 15)]] In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}}, the Ministry of Doctrine designated Sunomiya Shrine as the Shikinaisha, but reversed this decision the following year and designated Susaki Shrine instead, though the reasoning remains unclear. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine under the modern system. Located at a maritime chokepoint, the shrine maintained a custom until around {{jalink|1940|Q18633}}–{{jalink|1941|Q5231}} of collecting offerings from passing ships. In {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}, Mount Mitarai was designated a Chiba Prefectural Natural Monument as the “Susaki Shrine Natural Forest.” Today, the shrine is managed concurrently, with no resident priests. === Divine ranks === Chronology of divine ranks for “Amahiri-no-(To)-Me-no-Mikoto”: * {{jalink|Jōwa|Q1075605}} 9 ({{jalink|842|Q24730}}) Oct 2: Promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*Shoku Nihon Kōki*). * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 2 ({{jalink|852|Q30383}}) Aug 22: Promoted to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*). * {{jalink|Ten’an|Q1075611}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) Jan 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit to Senior Third Rank, Eighth Order of Merit (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*). === Precincts === [[File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main hall (Designated Cultural Property of Tateyama City)}}]] The main hall is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with copper roofing. Shrine tradition dates it to the {{jalink|Enpō|Q1145735}} era ({{jalink|1673|Q7617}}–{{jalink|1681|Q7645}}), though large-scale repairs occurred after the mid-Edo period. It is designated a Tangible Cultural Property of Tateyama City. The Zuijin Gate was built during the {{jalink|Hōei|Q1145262}} era, and 150 steps lead from behind it to the shrine buildings. Outside the beach torii stands a rounded stone called the “Sacred Stone,” said to be one of a pair dedicated from the Dragon Palace, the other enshrined at {{jalink|Awaguchi Shrine|Q59282598}} on the {{jalink|Miura Peninsula|Q536757}}. The Awaguchi stone represents “A-gyō,” while the Susaki stone represents “Un-gyō,” together guarding the entrance to Tokyo Bay{{refnest|group="Notes"|Information board.}}. Because the sea before the shrine is a key maritime route into Tokyo Bay, many votive plaques dedicated by ship captains for safe voyages were once seen before the war. After the shrine suffered major damage from the {{jalink|2019 Bōsō Peninsula Typhoon|Q67170176}} in {{jalink|2019|Q62609017}}, donations were received from various supporters, including the Tokyo talent agency {{jalink|Johnny & Associates|Q11728013|Johnny & Associates (then; now SMILE-UP.)}}, and used for reconstruction such as rebuilding torii of the Inari sub-shrine. A commemorative stone expressing gratitude for this support stands within the precincts. File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), haiden-2.jpg|Worship hall File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), keidaisha-1.jpg|Sub-shrine File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), keidaisha-2.jpg|Sub-shrine File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), shinmon.jpg|Zuijin Gate File:Susaki-jinja (Tateyama), shinseki.jpg|Sacred stone === Festivals === * Early February (Hatsuuma): Offering of the {{jalink|Susaki Minoko Odori|Q11556509|Susaki Dance}}. * Grand Festival ({{jalink|August 20|Q2824}}–{{jalink|August 21|Q2823}}): Portable shrine procession and Susaki Dance. === Cultural properties === === Chiba Prefecture–designated === * {{jalink|List of Chiba Prefecture Cultural Properties#Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11406175|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} ** {{jalink|Susaki Minoko Odori|Q11556509}} **: Consists of Miroku Dance and Kashima Dance. Designated June 9, {{jalink|1961|Q18051}}. * {{jalink|List of Chiba Prefecture Cultural Properties#Natural Monuments|Q11406175|Natural Monument}} ** Susaki Shrine Natural Forest **: A preserved natural forest on Mount Mitarai, designated September 29, {{jalink|1972|Q2476}}. === Tateyama City–designated === * Tangible Cultural Properties ** Susaki Shrine Main Hall – designated February 21, {{jalink|1967|Q18277}}. ** Three documents titled “Susaki Daimyōjin Engi” – designated August 26, {{jalink|1970|Q18275}}. * Tangible Folk Cultural Property ** Sacred hair – said to be the hair of the enshrined deity Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto. === Branch shrines === * Underwater branch shrine (within Hasama Underwater Park): Established to pray for prevention of maritime accidents. * {{jalink|Shinagawa Shrine|Q11418419}} ({{jalink|Shinagawa Ward|Q233495}}, {{jalink|Tokyo Metropolis|Q1490}}): Transferred by Minamoto no Yoritomo for maritime safety. * Edo branch: Said to have been transferred by {{jalink|Ōta Dōkan|Q386144}} as the guardian deity of Edo. === Access === '''Address''' * Susaki 1697, Tateyama City, Chiba Prefecture '''Transportation''' * From {{jalink|Tateyama Station|Q553883}} ({{jalink|JR East|Q499071}}{{jalink|Uchibō Line|Q1142964}}) ** Take {{jalink|JR Bus|Q1010222}} (Susaki Line) to “Susaki Shrine” stop (about 30 minutes) '''Nearby''' * {{jalink|Sunozaki Lighthouse|Q1087248}} * Yajiri Well: About 700 m north of the shrine; associated with a legend of Minamoto no Yoritomo. === References === === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} === Further reading === * Jirō Aida, *Medieval Checkpoints* (Une Shobō, 1943; Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011}}{{ill|lt=Tateyama City|Q646755|WD=Q646755}} Susaki 1344 |Coordinates = {{coord|34|58|5|N|139|45|29.60|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deity = {{ill|lt=Amahiritome-no-Mikoto|Q85879124|WD=Q85879124|lt=Amahiri-no-Me-no-Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (Major) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}
